region_of_origin: India (South Indian origin, widely served across India and in Indian restaurants globally)
food_subgroup: Soups and broths — spiced lentil-based soup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Mulligatawny Shorba. FOODON_03301710 (lentil soup) is the closest defensible parent category. The dish is a spiced lentil-based soup with South Indian origins.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW (legume-based soup) is the closest defensible parent. No specific Mulligatawny code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Mulligatawny Shorba or a sufficiently close soup subtype. Ontology gap acknowledged.

main_ingredients: red lentils (masoor dal) or toor dal

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Mulligatawny Shorba is approximately 300 g (one bowl), consistent with typical Indian restaurant soup portions served in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Mulligatawny Soup — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (adapted); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central lentil soup reference (SR Legacy #06215)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Mulligatawny Shorba as a restaurant dish. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (lentil-based soup / rasam / pepper water entries) and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy lentil soup (FDC #172451). The dish is a spiced lentil broth with moderate protein from lentils and optional meat. Values represent a mid-range estimate for the veg/non-veg composite as served in a restaurant context. Confidence is medium due to recipe variability.

gi_source.title: Lentil soup GI reference — University of Sydney GI Database; Lentils (Lens culinaris) GI = 29 ± 1 (boiled); analogous estimate applied to Mulligatawny Shorba
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mulligatawny Shorba. The GI is estimated at 30 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled lentils (GI ≈ 29) and lentil soup (GI range 28–44 across published studies). The spiced broth base and dilution with water/stock lower the effective GI relative to plain lentils. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 19.5) / 100 = 5.85, rounded to 5.9. Serving carbohydrate = 6.5 g/100g × 300 g serving = 19.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a 300 g restaurant serving delivers approximately 930 mg sodium, representing about 40% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to half a bowl, as the restaurant version of Mulligatawny Shorba can contribute substantially to daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated GI ≈ 30) driven by lentil-based composition; moderate dietary fibre (1.4 g/100 g) supports slower glucose absorption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mulligatawny Shorba is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI and lentil-derived protein and fibre content, though sodium and portion size should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (0.6 g/100 g) from ghee or coconut milk; beneficial lentil-derived soluble fibre and potassium; elevated sodium in restaurant preparation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The lentil base provides cardiovascular benefit through soluble fibre and potassium, but individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of the sodium load and request ghee-free or coconut-milk-free preparation where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mulligatawny Shorba (also spelled Mulligtwanny Shorba or Mulligatawny Soup) is a traditional South Indian spiced lentil soup that has been widely adopted across Indian cuisine and is popular in Indian restaurants throughout Singapore and globally. The name derives from the Tamil words 'milagu' (pepper) and 'thanni' (water), reflecting its origins as a peppery broth. The dish is prepared by simmering red lentils or toor dal with onion, tomato, garlic, ginger, black pepper, cumin, coriander, turmeric, and curry leaves in water or vegetable stock, then finishing with a tempering of ghee or oil and a squeeze of lemon juice. A non-vegetarian variant incorporates shredded chicken or lamb. The soup has a warm, earthy, mildly spicy flavour with a slightly tangy finish from lemon and tomato. Its texture ranges from a thin broth to a lightly thickened soup depending on the lentil ratio. Nutritionally, Mulligatawny Shorba provides moderate protein from lentils (approximately 3.2 g per 100 g), low fat, and a meaningful amount of dietary fibre and folate. Its low glycemic index (estimated GI ≈ 30) makes it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. The sodium content in restaurant preparations is elevated and warrants attention for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is typically served as a starter or light meal and is a staple on North and South Indian restaurant menus in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Mulligatawny Shorba as a restaurant dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN lentil-based soup and rasam entries and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy lentil soup data. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database lentil reference. Confidence is medium due to recipe variability between vegetarian and non-vegetarian variants and restaurant-specific preparation differences.
notes: 1. The food name in the input ('Mulligtwanny Shorba') contains a common misspelling; the canonical English name is 'Mulligatawny Shorba' or 'Mulligatawny Soup'. The misspelling is preserved as an alias for retrieval purposes. 2. The dish is offered in both vegetarian (lentil-only) and non-vegetarian (with chicken or lamb) variants; nutrient values represent a composite mid-range estimate. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reliably reported in ICMR-NIN lentil soup entries and is expected to be negligible in the vegetarian variant. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this food type. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this food type. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this food type. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in lentil-based soups per USDA SR Legacy reference. 8. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database lentil data (GI ≈ 29–30); no direct GI measurement for Mulligatawny Shorba was found in approved sources. 9. The dish is classified as Indian cuisine (South Indian origin) served in a Singapore restaurant context; multilingual names follow the Singaporean dish rule (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). 10. Dietary flags reflect the composite veg/non-veg nature; is_vegetarian and is_vegan are set to false to reflect the non-veg variant availability. 11. Halal status is set to true as the dish is commonly prepared halal in Singapore Indian restaurants, though consumers should verify with the specific restaurant.
